---
date: "2026-06-29"
---

## One Sandwich, Many Fillings

- Part 2 — the variance sandwich and three shapes for Omega

::: notes
Welcome back to Mentor Session Two, "Why your standard error is the whole ballgame," part two of four. In part one we watched the same beta hat of zero-point-four-two earn two opposite verdicts, just from a choice about the error matrix Omega. Now we open up the machine. The honest variance of an ordinary-least-squares estimator always has the same shape — a sandwich — and every standard-error flavor you have ever heard of is just a different guess about what goes in the middle. Once you see the sandwich, the whole zoo of robust, clustered, and HAC standard errors stops being a list of recipes and becomes one idea with three fillings.
:::

## The Variance Sandwich

- Honest variance: $(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\boldsymbol\Omega\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}$
- "Bread" $(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ is fixed; "filling" $\boldsymbol\Omega$ is unknown
- Classical SE assumes the fantasy diet $\boldsymbol\Omega = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}$
- We never re-fit $\hat\beta$ — the whole game is the middle

::: notes
Here is Chapter two-point-four in one breath. The honest variance of beta hat is always the sandwich: X-prime-X inverse, times X-prime Omega X, times X-prime-X inverse again. Think of the two X-prime-X-inverse terms on the outside as the bread — they are fixed by your design matrix and you never argue about them. The middle, X-prime Omega X, is the filling, and it contains Omega, the unknown error covariance matrix. The classical standard error is just this sandwich evaluated for one specific fantasy: Omega equals sigma-squared times the identity matrix, which says every error has the same variance and no two errors are correlated. We never re-fit beta hat. The entire game is estimating that middle filling without pretending Omega is sigma-squared I.
:::

## Three Real Shapes for Omega

- **Heteroskedastic** — unequal variances $\rightarrow$ robust / White (HC)
- **Block-correlated** — errors hold hands within groups $\rightarrow$ clustered
- **Time-banded** — this month echoes last $\rightarrow$ HAC
- In finance, $\boldsymbol\Omega = \sigma^2\mathbf{I}$ is essentially always false

::: notes
In finance the fantasy that Omega equals sigma-squared I is essentially always false, and it fails in three distinct ways, each a different shape for Omega. First, heteroskedasticity: bigger loans bring bigger surprises, so the variances along the diagonal are not equal. The fix is robust, or White, or H-C standard errors. Second, block correlation: errors hold hands within groups — the same firm, the same lender, the same state — so Omega has off-diagonal blocks. The fix is clustering. Third, time banding: this month's shock echoes into next month, so errors are correlated across nearby time periods. The fix there is a H-A-C, or heteroskedasticity-and-autocorrelation-consistent, estimator. Same sandwich, three different fillings.
:::

## Why Naive SEs Lie — and Which Way

- Positive within-group correlation $\rightarrow$ each obs carries *less* info than $N$ pretends
- Moulton: true Var $\approx$ naive Var $\times\,[\,1 + (n-1)\rho_x\rho_\varepsilon\,]$
- Damage scales with **cluster size** $n$ — a t of $6.0$ can honestly be $1.8$
- Naive SE almost always too *small*; t too *big* — overconfidence is default

::: notes
Why does ignoring this make the naive standard error wrong, and in which direction? Because positive within-group correlation means each observation carries less independent information than the raw count N pretends. The Moulton-style arithmetic from Chapter two-point-four is brutal: the true variance is approximately the naive variance times the quantity one plus, in parentheses, n minus one, times rho-x times rho-epsilon — where n is the cluster size and the two rhos measure correlation in the regressor and in the errors. The damage scales with cluster size. So the naive standard error is almost always too small, and the t-statistic too big. A t-statistic of six-point-zero can honestly be a one-point-eight. Overconfidence is the default failure mode. And notice the consequence: more observations stuffed inside your existing clusters do not buy you evidence — only more clusters do.
:::

## Key Takeaway

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
Every SE is the same sandwich $(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}\mathbf{X}'\boldsymbol\Omega\mathbf{X}(\mathbf{X}'\mathbf{X})^{-1}$ with a different filling $\boldsymbol\Omega$: heteroskedastic (robust), block (clustered), or time-banded (HAC). Positive within-group correlation makes naive SEs too **small**, so overconfidence is the default. Next: the panel case and Petersen's taxonomy.
:::

::: notes
To summarize part two: every standard error is the same sandwich, X-prime-X inverse times X-prime Omega X times X-prime-X inverse, with a different filling for Omega — heteroskedastic gives you robust, block-correlated gives you clustered, time-banded gives you H-A-C. Positive within-group correlation makes the naive standard error too small, so overconfidence is the default failure mode, and more observations inside existing clusters do not help — more clusters do. In part three we move to the setting where this gets genuinely subtle: the panel, where a firm effect and a time effect can both be present at once, and Petersen's taxonomy tells you what to do about it.
:::
